steps.dialogue: Remember when we split things into equal parts? Each piece was exactly the same size within one bar. Let's count the parts in each bar.  [event:68fa9056f56413ac] One, two parts... [delay] [event:68fa91e8e274738e] one, two, three parts... [delay] [event:68fa9270a341fe91] one, two, three, four parts. All equal within their own bar.
But when we compare [event:68fa9361415ccbfa] a bar with two equal parts to one with three equal parts or four equal parts, the pieces aren't all the same size.
Let's look at these five bars.[event:68fa96330219f34f]  They're all exactly the same size [event:68fa952209adc938], but they’re divided up differently.
Click one piece in each bar, any piece you want.

prompt.text: Click on one part in each of the bars.

step.dialogue: [event:6949c559637b59e1] Try it once more. Click one piece in each of the bars.
[event:68fa9bf0c9eb429d] We want to click one piece from each bar. Start with the first bar and work your way down.

[event:6949c4ada5c0fe9a] Let's work through this together: I'll click one piece in each bar - watch as I select one piece from the [event:exploration_task_hv] 2-part bar, then the 3-part bar, then 4, then 6, then 8.

on_correct.dialogue: The bars are the same size, but the pieces you selected are all different sizes.

steps.dialogue: Some of these pieces are bigger [event:68fa96d387cbb0c3], and some smaller[event:68fa96f333818847]. Even though the bars are all the same size, the pieces [event:68fa9f8adee7a7f8] you selected are all different sizes.
Let's look closely at all five pieces you highlighted. Which one is the smallest? Click the smallest piece.

prompt.text: Click on the bar that has the smallest pieces.

step.dialogue: Remember to count the parts in each bar. More parts means smaller pieces.
Let me help you compare. Watch [event:68fa9f8adee7a7f8] as the pieces get smaller - which bar has the tiniest piece?

[event:judgment_task_hv] Watch me trace this: [event:6949cb0d36c1be4e] 2 parts = big pieces, [event:6949cb30f7d6fe01] 4 parts = smaller, [event:6949cb6e6358a1c1] 8 parts = tiniest. I'll select the 8-part piece. [event:6949cd632bc1ec4b]

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. [event:68fa9d494ca5d1a0]The bar divided into the MOST parts has the smallest pieces. More parts means smaller pieces.

steps.dialogue: This pattern always works that way. [event:68fa9f8adee7a7f8]   If we're creating more equal pieces from the same whole, each piece gets smaller.

You learned that more parts [event:68faa0bb502613f3] means smaller pieces. Did you know that math experts have a word for fractions that show exactly ONE [event:68faa143f16b1f15] of those pieces? Let's learn more together...
